coredata.dc:description: All-solid-state batteries (ASSBs) are promising candidates for next-generation energy storage. However, realizing their potential requires an understanding of their underlying coupled, multiphysics behaviors. In an effort to understand these complex interactions, the present paper develops and applies a finite-element phase-field model that represents coupled electro-chemo-mechanical behaviors in composite ASSBs cathodes. The model predicts stress distributions as well as fracture and phase separations under several operating conditions. The results show that structural disintegration and the resulting loss of active surface area creates tortuous pathways for Li and Li-ion transport, contributing to capacity fade. The model is used to investigate the sensitivity of cell performance to different variables. The model evaluates the effects of electrode/electrolyte material properties, such as material stiffness and fracture toughness; microstructural characteristics, such as porosity and void distribution; and operating conditions such as charge/discharge rates and externally applied pressure. The voltage responses are validated using previously published experimental measurements. The model can be used to inform microstructural design and operating conditions that minimize or prevent mechanical damage during multiphysical interactions in ASSBs.

head.ce:title: Modeling coupled electro-chemo-mechanical phenomena within all-solid-state battery composite cathodes

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: All-solid-state batteries (ASSBs) are promising candidates for next-generation energy storage. However, realizing their potential requires an understanding of their underlying coupled, multiphysics behaviors. In an effort to understand these complex interactions, the present paper develops and applies a finite-element phase-field model that represents coupled electro-chemo-mechanical behaviors in composite ASSBs cathodes. The model predicts stress distributions as well as fracture and phase separations under several operating conditions. The results show that structural disintegration and the resulting loss of active surface area creates tortuous pathways for Li and Li-ion transport, contributing to capacity fade. The model is used to investigate the sensitivity of cell performance to different variables. The model evaluates the effects of electrode/electrolyte material properties, such as material stiffness and fracture toughness; microstructural characteristics, such as porosity and void distribution; and operating conditions such as charge/discharge rates and externally applied pressure. The voltage responses are validated using previously published experimental measurements. The model can be used to inform microstructural design and operating conditions that minimize or prevent mechanical damage during multiphysical interactions in ASSBs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: All-solid-state batteries (ASSBs) are increasingly recognized as front-runners in the quest to develop high-performance secondary batteries. These batteries have advantages over leading Li-ion technologies including: higher energy density, inherently improved safety, and the ability to operate at higher temperatures. Nevertheless, numerous challenges, such as degradation and capacity fade, must be addressed to achieve commercial viability (Schnell et al., 2018; Albertus et al., 2021; Janek and Zeier, 2023).

Fig. 1 illustrates an idealized rendering of the internal structure in an ASSBs composite cathode. As illustrated, Li
x
Ni0.8Mn0.1Co0.1O2 (NMC) secondary cathode particles are embedded within a polycrystalline Li
6
PS
5
Cl (LPSC) solid electrolyte matrix. Electronic conduction within the cathode is established with a percolating nanocarbon network. In ASSBs, crack growth is a prominent capacity-fading mechanism (Koerver et al., 2018; Ruess et al., 2020; Conforto et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021; Teo et al., 2022; Morino, 2022). When solid electrolytes are used instead of liquid electrolytes, the cathode-electrolyte interface is especially prone to the crack generation and void formation during fabrication (Zhang et al., 2023) and during operation (Noh et al., 2017; Shi et al., 2020; Bielefeld et al., 2020; Minnmann et al., 2021; Bielefeld et al., 2022; Minnmann et al., 2022).

Previous experimental and analytical studies have identified compositional and microstructural features and operating conditions that can substantially enhance battery performance (Noh et al., 2017; Shi et al., 2020; Bielefeld et al., 2020; Minnmann et al., 2021; Bielefeld et al., 2022; Minnmann et al., 2022). Depending on the fabrication process, the void distribution in the composite cathode structure can vary significantly, which in turn affects electrolyte conductivity, crack surface tortuosity, crack distribution, and overall cell performance. For example, during synthesis, external pressure can be applied to reach a desirable goal of 5% void volume fraction within the composite electrode structure (Kodama et al., 2020). Additionally, external pressure can be applied during operation to maintain contact between the electrode-electrolyte interfaces as sulfide-based solid electrolytes (e.g., LPSC) are sufficiently malleable so as to maintain interfacial contact under external pressure. With cathode particles such as NMC, undesirable primary-secondary morphology and intragranular fracture can result in increased tortuosity within the active material. Additional resistances in the active material can be mitigated or eliminated by incorporating single-crystal or highly aligned microstructures (Wang et al., 2020; Doerrer et al., 2021; Han et al., 2021; Trevisanello et al., 2021).

Physics-based ASSBs models have been developed previously at the particle-scale (Taghikhani et al., 2020, 2021b; Fathiannasab et al., 2021; Ai et al., 2022; Singh and Pal, 2022; Bielefeld et al., 2022; Rezaei et al., 2023; Farzanian et al., 2023) and cell-scale (Bucci et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2019; Boyce et al., 2022; Lopata et al., 2023). Additionally, Newman-type pseudo-two-dimensional (P2D) and single-particle models have been widely used to predict cell performance in traditional Li-ion batteries (Fuller et al., 1994; Doyle et al., 1996; Guo et al., 2010; Jokar et al., 2016; Mai et al., 2019). However, P2D models significantly simplify factors such as structural anisotropy, lattice deformations, and associated particle fracture and phase separations, making them less appropriate for studying ASSBs. In ASSBs, accurate cell-performance predictions depend significantly on the chemo-mechanical response (typically neglected in P2D models), which highly depends on the cell’s microstructure and interconnected electro-chemo-mechanical behaviors. Importantly, these multi-physics models depend heavily on the actual microstructure that can instantiate structural damage under varying structural (material properties, porosity, electrode/electrolyte crystallinity, particle orientation, etc.) and environmental or operating conditions (external pressure, temperature, charging rates, etc.). These heterogeneous models enable accurate performance predictions and can suggest modifications of electrode structures and operating conditions in ASSBs.

Chemo-mechanical battery degradation has been investigated previously across different scales. Some studies concentrate on evaluating local stress (Zhang et al., 2007; Malavé et al., 2014; Wu et al., 2016; Taghikhani et al., 2020; Fathiannasab et al., 2021; Farzanian et al., 2023), while others explore the complexities of fracture and particle disintegration. Researchers have implemented techniques such as cohesive zone modeling (Sun et al., 2016; Bucci et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2017; Xu and Zhao, 2018; Zhang et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2019; Bai et al., 2020), phase-field fracture (Singh and Pal, 2020; Rezaei et al., 2021; Ai et al., 2022; Singh and Pal, 2022; Boyce et al., 2022; Rezaei et al., 2023; Taghikhani et al., 2023), continuum damage (Allen et al., 2021), effective springs (Müller et al., 2018; Taghikhani et al., 2021b; Barai, 2022) and extended-finite-elements (Zhu et al., 2020) to predict electrode cracking. Using energy-minimization principles, the phase-field fracture technique is capable of modeling aspects of fracture such as crack nucleation, growth, merging and branching without any initial cracks (Miehe et al., 2010a,b; Wu et al., 2020). Additionally, because of their variational formulation, phase-field models are well suited for generalization and adaption to other multiphysics applications (Miehe et al., 2015).

The present research builds on and extends previously published models. Specifically, Bielefeld et al. (2020, 2022) have developed heterogeneous models to evaluate the ASSBs performance. They investigated the effects of particle-size distributions, concentration-dependent material properties, and composite-electrode porosity. The current work extends these models to incorporate coupled electro-chemo-mechanical behavior, phase separations, and fracture. The proposed two-dimensional (2D) model incorporates phase-field fracture mechanics to predict disintegration of electrodes, electrolytes, and interfaces. Similar to Bielefeld et al. (2022), the simulations are validated based on experimental voltage-capacity observations with different charging rates (Ruess et al., 2020; Trevisanello et al., 2021; Conforto et al., 2021). The present model is then used to investigate cell performance dependence on cathode particle size, cathode-particle crystallinity, solid electrolyte material properties, structural voids, and operating conditions (e.g., pressure and charging rates).

The present, fully coupled electro-chemo-mechanical formulation provides a framework that can accommodate structural anisotropy and misaligned crystallographic interfaces, within secondary NMC particles and at electrode-electrolyte interfaces. The governing equations are derived from considering contributions from (1) fracture energy
Ψ

, (2) elastic strain energy
Ψ
e
, and (3) chemical energy
Ψ
c
. The governing equations ensure (1) conservation of Li species, (2) conservation of charge, (3) conservation of momentum, and (4) thermodynamically consistent fracture energy dissipation.

The phase-field fracture formulation was developed as an approximation to the variational formulation of fracture, to circumvent difficulties in the representation of a sharp crack boundary (Bourdin et al., 2000). In the phase-field formulation, the crack is smeared over a characteristic width,
ℓ
0
, using a crack-order parameter field ξ , which is coupled to mechanical constitutive relations. As the characteristic width approaches zero, Griffith’s theory is recovered (Griffith, 1921). The present model is developed so that no traction can be carried in regions where
ξ = 1
. The material remains completely intact in regions where
ξ = 0
.

Fig. 3 illustrates the cathode half-cell microstructure of a heterogeneous solid-state battery. The figure is edited and phase-segmented using Adobe Illustrator and im2mesh. The porosity and particle sizes have adjusted to match values reported by Bielefeld et al. (2022).

Prior literature suggests alternative forms of chemo-mechanical flux, each based on differing assumptions about the transport mechanism, such as substitutional (Larché and Cahn, 1982; Bohn et al., 2013), interstitial (Zhang et al., 2007), or a combination of both (Xu and Zhao, 2018; Haftbaradaran et al., 2011; Taghikhani et al., 2021b, 2023). These combinations can result in asymmetric transport stoichiometry between Li ions and voids (Bohn et al., 2013). The current model adopts a fully substitutional approach, ensuring a symmetric transport process. Additionally, the second term in Eq. (24) represents the contribution of stress to the flux. Based on the current formulation, the fully lithiated (and delithiated) lattice site states are stress-free.

The current model assumes that the solid electrolyte stoichiometry remains unchanged upon charge or discharge (De Klerk et al., 2016). Thus, the model assumes a constant Li-ion concentration within the solid electrolyte phase. However, the model does consider spatial variations of the electrostatic potential within the electrolyte phase. Consequently, the location of the active material particle in the composite cathode structure affects the electrochemical performance. The effects of a percolating electrically conductive carbon-binder phase are implicitly captured as part of the composite electrolyte structure. While carbon additives improve the electronic percolation, detrimental decomposition reactions at the interface of carbon-based conductive agent and solid electrolyte can contribute to capacity fade (Walther et al., 2020). The current model assumes that electronic conduction is not a limiting factor in the composite cathode performance and assumes a relatively high electronic conductivity for NMC particles.

Fig. 4 illustrates the electrochemical and mechanical boundary condition assumptions to constrain the governing differential–algebraic equations. The transport related boundary conditions are marked in red and mechanical boundary conditions are shown in gray.

The initial Li concentration in the NMC particles is considered to be uniform at
x = 0 . 97

This formulation incorporates the effect of strain energy on the reaction rates.

The two-dimensional mechanics are simplified from 3D by assuming a plane-strain condition with no chemical expansion in the out-of-plane direction. This simplification provides a more accurate representation of the state of stress in 3D particles (Taghikhani et al., 2024).

The phase-field parameter is initially set to be uniformly zero, representing an undamaged structure. Fig. 5 shows that critical energy release rates
G
c
are different in electrode, electrolyte and interfaces. The critical energy release rates are distributed by solving the phase-field equation. The length scale parameter is selected to match the characteristic width
ℓ
0
used in the phase-field fracture formulation. The critical values are selected as
G
c
= 2 . 79
J m−2 for LPSC,
G
c
= 2 . 50
J m−2 for NMC, and
G

= 1 . 00
J m−2 for the electrode-electrolyte interfaces. Although the present modes assumes constant critical energy release rates, there is evidence that they can depend on Li concentration and cycling (Xu et al., 2017; Taghikhani et al., 2023).

The system of fully coupled differential–algebraic equations is solved numerically using the finite-element method embedded in COMSOL Multiphysics. The quadratic triangular mesh structure consists of approximately 3.5 million degrees of freedom. Considering the size of the problem, the model is solved using a segregated solver.

The value of length scale parameter
ℓ
0
is critical in strength-dominated phase-field problems, particularly in scenarios with small or non-existent initial defects. In the absence of empirical data on strength of materials, the parameter selection relies on reasonable approximations aligned with available estimates in the literature (Xu et al., 2019; Bai et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2016). The selected parameter aims to balance accuracy with computational efficiency, while recognizing the inherent limitations of the model. Additionally, the relationship between the length scale parameter and mesh in phase field fracture models is crucial for accurate simulations. The interfacial boundaries are most susceptible to fracture due to lower fracture toughness. Fig. 6 shows that the mesh is refined at the interfaces such that the maximum element mesh size
h
max
is less than half of the crack length-scale parameter (
h
max
< 0 . 5
ℓ
0
). It is worth noting that despite the maximum element size, the elements are generally about 4–5 smaller than the length scale parameter in the model (c.f., Fig. A.2). This condition ensures an accurate finite-element approximation within the specified constraints (Miehe et al., 2010b; Kristensen et al., 2021).

Constant electro-chemo-mechanical model parameters are listed in the Table 1. It is important to note that these parameters are assumed to be constant to reduce the computational cost and complexities of the developed heterogeneous model. However, in reality, the properties may vary with concentration. Key concentration-dependent material properties in the current model include the diffusion coefficient, exchange current density, and equilibrium potential, which are plotted in the Fig. A.1, (Ruess et al., 2020; Bielefeld et al., 2022). Moreover, the current model assumes isotropic behavior inside cathode particles (
β
i j
=
β
iso
δ
i j
), to reduce the computational cost. With this assumption, the model does not resolve the orientation of individual primary particles. Instead, the average coupled chemo-mechanical behavior of the polycrystalline particle with the isotropic chemical-expansion coefficient is considered. The decohesion of primary-secondary cathode particles was previously analyzed at the particle level (Taghikhani et al., 2023) and remains a subject of ongoing and future investigation.

In traditional Li-ion batteries with a liquid electrolyte, electrolyte can penetrate into cracks and activate the open crack faces (Ruess et al., 2020). However, in the current ASSBs model, it is assumed that fracture does not lead to activation of new surfaces, as the solid electrolyte cannot infiltrate the cracks.

The model is applied to study the effects of electro-chemo-mechanical properties and operating conditions on cell capacity. To validate the results, the model size and electro-chemical material properties are primarily selected based on those reported by Bielefeld et al. (2022). The half-cell 2D model consists of a (40 × 40)
μ m
2
composite cathode structure with a
10
μ m
solid-electrolyte separator and a
1
μ m
current collector (Fig. 3). The particle and void distributions are random, with variable void sizes and related active surface areas.

Fig. 7 shows predicted and measured polarization responses with respect to capacity for 0.02 C, 0.1 C and 5 C charging. The model assumes a uniformly applied external pressure of
p = 40
MPa
on the left boundary (Fig. 4). The voltage profiles quite reasonably resemble the experimental work reported by Janek and coworkers (Ruess et al., 2020; Trevisanello et al., 2021; Conforto et al., 2021). Nevertheless, the simulations consistently over-predict the capacity for all three charging rates. Although the trends are well predicted, the quantitative discrepancies are likely due to model simplifications, including using a 2D model, the assumption of isotropic behavior in cathode particles, and neglecting factors such as randomly oriented primary-secondary morphology, inter-granular fracture, and formation of resistive cathode-electrolyte interphase (CEI) layer. These phenomena are subjects of future studies. Although the model itself is fully capable of representing three-dimensional geometries, the computation times are significant as compared to two-dimensional studies.

As expected, the capacity is significantly higher at lower C-rates. Also, consistent with the findings of Bielefeld et al. (2022), the discrepancies between simulation and experiment increases at higher C-rates. This behavior might be attributed to the faster formation CEI layer at higher charging rates.

Li concentration and first-principal stress distribution profiles are useful measures to investigate chemo-mechanical behavior in ASSBs. Fig. 8 shows (a) voltage-capacity, (b) concentration, and (c) stress profiles at the end of 0.1 C charge. The structure is compressed with
p = 40
MPa
pressure during the operation. Due to the lower fracture toughness, the cracks are mainly concentrated at the electrode-electrolyte interfaces. Despite the relatively slow 0.1 C rate, the Li concentration profile ranges from
x = 0 . 25
to
x = 0 . 70
intercalation fraction. The void distribution and crack propagation are the main factors contributing to the high concentration gradients. As highlighted in the magnified call-out, the reaction ceases at the fractured interfaces. This behavior leads to higher Li concentration distribution near the cracks and contributes to the high concentration gradients. The stress is highest at the electrode-electrolyte interfaces (Fig. 8c). Additionally the stress concentration at the crack-tips are illustrated in the stress call-out.

To provide a basis for comparison, the model is first exercised without external pressure. Fig. 9a shows voltage-capacity profiles at a 0.1 C rate without external pressure. The simulation profile predicts sudden jumps, which are not observed experimentally. The abrupt changes in the profile are caused by interfacial crack growth. Fig. 9b, c shows false-color maps of the damage parameter ξ prior to and after the voltage jumps, respectively. The fracture surfaces result in loss of active surface area and reduced capacity. Additionally, the fractures create a more tortuous pathway for the Li-ion transport, further contributing to capacity loss. The fracture effects are exaggerated in a 2D model because the cracks propagate infinitely in the out-of-plane direction.

Fig. 10a compares the voltage-capacity profiles for the structures with no applied pressure,
p = 40
MPa
pressure, and experimental results reported by Bielefeld et al. (2022) under a 0.1 C charge. Removing the pressure leads to a significant decrease in capacity. Fig. 10b, c show the crack distribution at the end of charge. The application of external pressure induces a more compressive stress state, which consequently limits fracture compared to the no-pressure scenario. The magnified views directly compare two regions of interest, illustrating that the external pressure reduces the overall fracture. The reduction in fracture, leading to more active surface areas and improved pathways for Li-ion transport, significantly increases the capacity.

It is notable that the present model has limitations in evaluating the effects of changing external pressure. Modifying the pressure results in only slightly changes to the voltage profiles. To capture this effect, future models should include a larger region of interest or incorporate the fracture mechanism within the polycrystalline NMC particles.

The present study focuses primarily on modeling a sulfide-based electrolyte (LPSC). However, oxide-based garnets are also widely regarded as promising alternatives for solid-state electrolytes (Krauskopf et al., 2019; Kim and Rupp, 2020). Sulfide-based electrolytes offer advantages such as high lithium-ion conductivity and more compliant structures compared to garnets. The garnets, on the other hand, exhibit superior stability in contact with lithium metal anodes. Fig. 11 compares the voltage-capacity profiles of composite cathode structures with La
3
Li
7
O12Zr
2
(LLZO) and LPSC solid electrolytes under 0.1 C charging rate and
p = 40
MPa
external pressure. The sulfide-based composite cathode experiences less fracture compared to garnets due to the compliant structure of LPSC. LLZO, on the other hand, exhibits more pronounced fracture at higher voltages. Consequently, the present model predicts that sulfide-based composite cathode exhibit better performance and achieve a higher capacity.

The current study investigates the impact of interfacial fracture toughness on capacity. However, measuring this needed material property can be challenging. For example, measuring the interfacial fracture toughness involves complicated methods like using a nano-indenter to induce blistering under high residual stress and measuring the blister size or double cantilever beam measurement. In this modeling study, the composite cathode is charged at 0.1 C, and no external pressure is imposed to isolate the effect of interfacial fracture toughness. Fig. 12 illustrates the voltage-capacity profiles for three simulations with interfacial fracture toughness values of
G
c
= 0 . 5 , 1
, and 2 J m−2. While the profiles closely align at the lower voltages, they diverge considerably at higher voltages. This study suggests that adding a coating layer to strengthen the bond between the electrode and electrolyte could substantially improve the capacity.

To study the influence of cycling on the system, a cycling protocol consisting of a 1 C constant-current charge, followed by 300 s constant-voltage hold, and 0.1 C constant-voltage discharge is simulated. Fig. 13 compares the cycling voltage-capacity profiles under 5 MPa and 40 MPa external pressures in the first three cycles. Both models reach the maximum capacity of 186 mAh g−1. At higher C-rates, the Li transport can become the rate-limiting phenomena. The simulation predicts that the most significant capacity-loss occurs during the first cycle, as the cracked surfaces reduce the active interfaces and thus impede the Li-ion transport. Capacity retention improves in the following cycles, which is consistent with the experimental findings (Doerrer et al., 2021). The polarization responses for the second and third cycle overlap significancy. Although both models perform similarly, the 40 MPa case has slightly higher capacity at the end of the second cycle. Considering the computational limitations, limited available material properties and the scope of cycling, The cycling fatigue and degradation of fracture toughness is not incorporated in the current work.

ASSBs composite cathode porosity can range from approximately 6% to 30%, depending on the material and fabrication process (Bielefeld et al., 2020). The effect of voids on the cell performance depends on the void size and distribution. Fig. 14a illustrates the predicted 0.1 C polarization response for two structures, one with no pores and one 15% porosity. As may be expected, removing the voids increases capacity due to higher active surface areas. Fig. 14b, c compare the cracked surfaces at the end of 0.1 C charge. The porous structure exhibits significantly less fracture, since the pores act as a stress relieving interface. Thus, increased porosity is expected to limit crack growth. While voids inherently reduce initial contact area between the cathode particles and solid electrolyte particles, they can reduce cycle-induced fracture which results in improved first-cycle Couloumbic efficiencies by inhibiting crack propagation within the composite cathode structure. Therefore, the composite cathode porosity presents a complex trade-off, in which optimizing pore structure is key to achieving higher capacities in ASSBs.

A finite-element phase-field model is developed to predict fully coupled electro-chemo-mechanical behavior within composite LPSC-NMC all-solid-state-battery electrodes. Although the model is fully capable of representing three-dimensional microstructures, the present paper reports results in a two-dimensional setting using a plane-strain assumption. Practical limitations for needed computational resources make full three-dimensional simulations impractical for parametric studies. The modeled microstructure is based on previously published SEM images of a representative cathode, including voids (Doerrer et al., 2021). The model predictions are consistent with previously published measurements of voltage as a function of capacity under a range of external pressures and charging conditions (Conforto et al., 2021).

The simulations predict transient Li concentrations, stress, strain, and displacement fields throughout the cathode microstructure. The phase-field model predicts a damage parameter that represents crack formation and growth, as well as electrode-electrolyte interface separations. The model is exercised to evaluate the effects of cycling rates, externally applied pressure, porosity, and material properties. Expectedly, the half-cell capacity increases with higher C-rates. Aligned with the literature reports, the most severe capacity loss occurs in the first cycle as the cracks create a more tortuous path for Li transport. Regarding the electrolyte properties, LPSC performs slightly better performance compared to LLZO, due to more compliant structure, and higher ionic conductivity. Additionally, coating the electrode-electrolyte interface can increase interfacial strength and fracture toughness, reducing the phase separations and leading to markedly superior cell performance. The results indicate that applying pressure during operation leads to a general more compressive state of stress, which can significantly reduce fracture and improve battery performance. Also, while reducing porosity can enhance the cell performance, it can also intensify fracture propagation as the stress-relieving pores are eliminated.

The paper reports representative results that provide insights for developing and improving composite ASSBs electrode structures. The predictions depend on fundamental materials properties, some of which are yet to be measured and validated. Moreover, even for nominally the same battery, microscale electrode-electrolyte geometric details vary from one cell to the next. Thus, a model such as the present one, provides the means to gather important qualitative insights that assist design objectives and fabrication processes.
